coredata.dc:title: Fluid-induced piezoelectric field enhanced photocatalytic antibiotic removal over nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/Bi2WO6@polyvinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene membrane in aqueous environments

coredata.dc:description: Enhancing the charge separation efficiency of photocatalysts is crucial to their catalytic activity, which is still challenging. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were combined with Bi2WO6 to construct an N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite, which was loaded onto the surface of polyvinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene (PVDF-HFP) membrane to design a flexible, porous and hydrophilic N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PVDF-HFP photocatalytic membrane. Piezo-response force microscopy (PFM) and the maximum effective piezoelectric coefficient (d33) measurements demonstrate that the PVDF-HFP membrane has favorable piezoelectric properties. Besides, fluid-induced mechanical energy can generate a piezoelectric field within the PVDF-HFP membrane. Theoretical calculations indicate that the difference in work function at the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite interface creates an inherent electric field. Therefore, the synergistic effect of the two electric fields improves the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated carriers in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite. The membrane effectively removed 85.3 % of oxytetracycline (OTC) under the synergistic driving of water flow (900 r/min) and visible light, surpassing the results of only water flow (34.4 %) and visible light (63.1 %). Furthermore, the degradation performance of the membrane towards OTC remains almost unchanged after multiple recycles, highlighting its favorable reusability. This work addresses the issue of powdery catalysts in recovering for practical applications and underlines the potential of integrating with natural low-frequency water flows to purify organic-contaminated wastewater.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enhancing the charge separation efficiency of photocatalysts is crucial to their catalytic activity, which is still challenging. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were combined with Bi2WO6 to construct an N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite, which was loaded onto the surface of polyvinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene (PVDF-HFP) membrane to design a flexible, porous and hydrophilic N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PVDF-HFP photocatalytic membrane. Piezo-response force microscopy (PFM) and the maximum effective piezoelectric coefficient (d33) measurements demonstrate that the PVDF-HFP membrane has favorable piezoelectric properties. Besides, fluid-induced mechanical energy can generate a piezoelectric field within the PVDF-HFP membrane. Theoretical calculations indicate that the difference in work function at the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite interface creates an inherent electric field. Therefore, the synergistic effect of the two electric fields improves the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated carriers in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite. The membrane effectively removed 85.3 % of oxytetracycline (OTC) under the synergistic driving of water flow (900 r/min) and visible light, surpassing the results of only water flow (34.4 %) and visible light (63.1 %). Furthermore, the degradation performance of the membrane towards OTC remains almost unchanged after multiple recycles, highlighting its favorable reusability. This work addresses the issue of powdery catalysts in recovering for practical applications and underlines the potential of integrating with natural low-frequency water flows to purify organic-contaminated wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics, as generally employed drugs for treating diseases, have been discharged into water environments without proper treatment due to abuse and improper handling, resulting in antibiotic resistance genes and bacterial resistance, as well as disrupting the balance of ecosystems [1,2]. Antibiotic pollution has become a major environmental problem facing the world. The photocatalytic technology driven by sunlight has received widespread attention in antibiotic pollution control due to its advantages of green energy saving, simple operation, and mild reaction conditions [3,4]. As far as known, the key step in photocatalysis technology is the design of photocatalysts with wide spectral response, high photogenerated charge separation efficiency, and strong surface redox ability [5,6]. Among various photocatalysts, Bi2WO6 is a representative perovskite oxide with a typical layered structure, favorable stability, low toxicity, band gap around 2.75 eV and low valence band (VB) position,all of which contribute to its suitability for photocatalytic removal of antibiotics [7,8]. However, the practical applications of single Bi2WO6 in degrading antibiotics are limited by several factors such as low absorption of sunlight, high electron-holes recombination and low mineralization rate [9,10].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbonaceous nanomaterials consisting of sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon cores and surface oxygen-containing functional groups [11–13]. Compared with metal and semiconductor materials, the CQDs have the advantages of simple preparation, low cost, and non-toxicity. At the same time, their up-conversion properties can widen the range of light absorption and promote the generation of photogenerated carriers in photocatalytic materials [14]. Referring to the principle of minimum energy, when there is a definite change in Fermi level between CQDs and inorganic semiconductors, an interface energy barrier can be formed. They can be utilized as a medium for storing/transferring electrons on the semiconductor conduction band (CB), capturing electrons and facilitating the separation of photoproduced carriers [15,16]. For example, Si et al. [17] found that the g-C3N4 system loaded with CQDs can reduce the recombination of photoproduced carriers and increase light absorption, resulting in a 14-fold increase in the degradation performance of tetracycline over CQDs/g-C3N4 system compared to the bare g-C3N4 under visible light exposure, providing new ideas for the design of green and efficient photocatalytic systems. Ren et al. [18] summarized the key role of CQDs in enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency of semiconductor materials, furnishing a reference for a deeper comprehension of the mechanism of carbon-based photocatalytic materials for antibiotics removal. Recent research works have shown that the strategy of designing green and efficient photocatalytic systems using CQDs combined with semiconductor materials is feasible. However, the practical application of these nanoscale powdery photocatalytic materials in water purification still faces some problems such as unsatisfactory charge separation efficiency and difficult recovery from solution [19,20]. Therefore, designing photocatalysts with high charge separation efficiency and convenient recycling is much needed to promote the practical applications of photocatalytic materials.

PVDF-HFP is one of the most common linear polymers, whose molecular chain is composed only of C, F and H atoms [21]. It was found that the PVDF-HFP possesses excellent chemical resistance, heat resistance, flexibility and piezoelectricity, and has shown wide applications in sensors, energy harvesting, storage devices and antibacterial fields [22,23]. For example, Zhou et al. [24] reported a Bi0.5Na0.5TiO3/PVDF piezoelectric photocatalytic membrane, which significantly boosted the degradation performance of Rhodamine B under the synergistic effect of light illumination and ultrasonication. Similarly, Fazli et al. [25] developed a SnO2/g-C3N4/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric photocatalyst, which exhibited efficient photocatalytic degradation of polystyrene under ultrasound. The F atoms have higher electronegativity than the H and C atoms, resulting in a strong electric dipole moment in PVDF-HFP, which endows it with favorable piezoelectric properties [26]. The PVDF-HFP membrane emerges as a promising substrate for piezoelectric photocatalytic materials to eliminate pollutants in water. However, most of the previously reported PVDF-HFP-based piezoelectric photocatalysts require high-frequency ultrasound to achieve favorable catalytic activity, which results in additional energy consumption and noise pollution. Therefore, developing green, energy-saving and efficient PVDF-HFP-based catalysts is of great significance in promoting their practical applications.

Following the insights from the above-reported research, as shown in Scheme 1 , we constructed a novel N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 composite material, which was further embedded into a PVDF-HFP polymer membrane, resulting in the design of a flexible, porous and hydrophilic N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PVDF-HFP photocatalytic membrane (N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM). The PFM and d33 tests evidence that the PVDF-HFP membrane has favorable piezoelectric properties. The piezoelectric field generated by the water flow-induced PVDF-HFP membrane and the internal electric field at the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite interface synergistically enhances the separation and migration of photoproduced carriers in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6. Driven by the synergistic effect of weak water flow and visible light, the designed membrane effectively removed 85.3 % of OTC pollutants and demonstrated favorable recyclability.

All of these chemicals were analytical grade and employed as received without any further purification. Oxytetracycline (OTC, 98 %), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO, 97 %), 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidinol (TEMP, 97 %), poly(vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene) (PVDF-HFP, Mw∼455,000), N-Methylpyrrolidone (NMP, 99 %), furfuryl alcohol (FFA, 98 %), ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, 25 %) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9 %) were supplied from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O, 99 %), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 99 %), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4, 99.5 %), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 99.5 %), ascorbic acid (AA, 99.0 %) and hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, 99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagents (Shanghai, China). The discarded corn stalks were supplied from rural areas in Xianyang City, Shaanxi Province, China.

Step (1): 0.3 g of corn stover powder and 0.1 g of urea were solubilized in 60 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 1 h. Then, the obtained solution was transferred into a 100 mL high-pressure vessel and kept at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After completing the reaction, the mixture was filtered 3 times employing a 50 nm filter membrane to get a vitreous solution. Then, the solution was oven-dried at 80 ℃ for 12 h to generate N-CQDs powder. Step (2): 0.33 g of Na2WO4·2H2O powder, 0.97 g of Bi (NO3)3·5H2O powder, and 50 mg of CTAB surfactant were dissolved together in 75 mL of ultrapure water magnetically stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, 21 mg of the synthesized N-CQDs from step (1) was added to the above mixture and mixed under a magnetic stirrer for 1 h. Then the homogenous mixture was transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor and heated at 120 ℃ for 24 h. After finishing the reaction, the resulting product was washed 5 times using anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water, and oven-dried at 80 ℃ for 10 h to generate the desired N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 photocatalyst. The preparation of the pristine Bi2WO6 is similar to the above procedures, except for the addition of N-CQDs.

Initially, 0.5 g of PVDF-HFP was added to 9.8 mL of NMP, followed by 200 μL of NH3·H2O. The combination was agitated at 60 ℃ water bath for 3 h to create a translucent gelatinous liquid. Subsequently, 20 mg of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 catalyst prepared above was added and stirred constantly for 30 min. Afterwards, the mixture was moved to a glass culture dish with a diameter of 65 mm and a layer of ultrapure water was cast on the surface for phase transformation. After 30 min, the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM was removed from the glass substrate, washed three times with ultrapure water, and dried overnight at 40 ℃ to produce N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PVDF-HFP membrane (labeled as N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM).

The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) micrograph of samples was obtained using a Talos F200i field emission electron microscope (FEI, USA) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The surface morphology of samples was measured using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) with an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a Rigaku D/Max-2400 X-ray diffractometer with CuKαradiation (1.54056 Å). The piezoelectric response of membranes was evaluated using a piezoelectric force microscope (Agilent 5500, USA), with an applied alternating current voltage of ± 10 V. UV–visible diffuse reflection spectra (UV–vis DRS) were obtained using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cary 5000, Agilent, USA) with BaSO4 as the substrate. The membrane's Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using a Brucker spectrometer (Verte70V, Germany). The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were collected using Bruker (A300, Germany) equipment with a modulation frequency of 20.0 GHz. The contact angle of the as-obtained membrane was estimated by employing a contact angle tester (LAUDA Scientific, LSA60, Germany). An electrochemical system (CHI 660E, Shanghai ChenHua, China) was used to conduct the transient photocurrent plots and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS). Using a 300 W Xenon lamp as the light source, a standard three-electrode was submerged in a 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 electrolyte solution. The working electrode was a fluorine-doped tin oxide conductive glass (1 × 1 cm), whereas the reference and counter electrodes were made of platinum sheet/calomel electrodes.

The prepared N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM were soaked in OTC solution (i.e., 50 mL, 20 mg/L) and stirred in the dark condition for 40 min until reaching adsorption–desorption equilibrium. As revealed in Fig. S1, the photocatalytic degradation of OTC was conducted employing a 300 W xenon lamp (cut-off filter, λ ≥ 420 nm), during which the mechanical stirring rate was controlled by adjusting the speed of the magnetic stirrer (0–900 r/min). At a designated time, the concentration of OTC in the collected 3 mL solution was analyzed employing a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer, with the maximum absorption wavelength of OTC set at 355 nm. After completing OTC degradation, the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM were ultrasonically cleaned with purified water and anhydrous ethanol, and the cleaned N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM were collected for recycling. The above steps were repeated several times to evaluate the recyclability of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM photocatalytic membrane. The residual concentration of OTC was calculated employing the following equation (Eq. (1)):

where C 0 denotes the initial concentration of OTC and C represents its concentration at any time (t); A 0 refers to the initial absorbance, and A represents the absorbance at any time (t).

The Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package was used to run density functional theory (DFT) calculations [27]. The projected augmented wave approach was employed in conjunction with the generalized gradient approximation for the exchange–correlation functional, specifically utilizing the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof functional, and modified by the DFT-D2 method for dispersion corrections [28]. The initial model of the composite structure, consisting of the N-CQDs (100) plane and the Bi2WO6 (100) plane, was constructed using AB-stacked 5 × 5 supercells. To minimize interlayer interactions, a vacuum layer of 20 Å was introduced along the c-axis direction. The plane-wave cut-off energy was set at 550 eV. For structural optimization and self-consistent calculations, k-point meshes of 3 × 3 × 1 and 7 × 7 × 1 were used to sample the Brillouin zone, respectively. The convergence criterion for energy and force were chosen at 10−5 eV and 0.01 eV/Å, respectively. The work function (Φ) was calculated employing the following equation (Eq. (2)):
(2)
Φ =
E
v a c
-
E F
where Φ is the work function (in electron volts, eV), Evac is the vacuum level (the energy level of a free electron in the vacuum) and E F is the Fermi level (the highest occupied energy level in the material).

The TEM image of N-CQDs derived from discarded corn stover is shown in Fig. 1 (a), which presents a spherical shape with totally dispersed particles and no aggregation of N-CQDs. The HR-TEM micrograph (Fig. 1b) of N-CQDs shows obvious lattice stripes with a lattice distance of 0.212 nm, which can be assigned to the lattice distance of graphite carbon (100) planes [29]. The size distribution histogram (Fig. 1c) of N-CQDs shows a mean particle size of 3.4 nm, with a size distribution range of 1.88–4.93 nm. The aforementioned findings demonstrate that highly crystalline N-CQDs were prepared using waste corn stalks as raw materials through a hydrothermal strategy.

To identify the synthesized Bi2WO6 phase, XRD analysis was performed at room temperature. As revealed in Fig. 1(d), all diffraction peaks are attributed to Bi2WO6, and no diffraction peaks of N-CQDs were detected in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6, possibly because of the relative content of N-CQDs being less than 5 %. TEM characterization was performed on N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 to demonstrate the existence of N-CQDs. As presented in Fig. 1(e and f), the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 exhibit an irregular nano-spherical morphology, characterized by numerous small N-CQD particles on the surface (denoted by the red circles). After enlarging Fig. 1(f), an HR-TEM image as depicted in Fig. 1(g) was obtained, which displays two distinct lattice stripes on the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 surface. The region with a lattice distance of 0.212 nm matches the lattice distance of the N-CQDs (100) plane, the region with a lattice distance of 0.275 nm matches the lattice distance of the Bi2WO6 (200) crystal plane [30,31]. In addition, the element distribution map (Fig. 1h) shows that Bi, W, O, C and N atoms are uniformly distributed on N-CQDs/Bi2WO6, further proving the successful combination of N-CQDs and Bi2WO6.

The light absorption ability of N-CQDs, Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 was evaluated by UV–vis DRS spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 2 (a), compared with Bi2WO6, the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 exhibits improved light absorption capacity ranging from 400 to 800 nm. The optical band gap (Eg) of Bi2WO6 was estimated to be 2.73 eV using the Kubelka–Munk equation (Fig. 2b). The XPS-VB spectrum of Bi2WO6 is displayed in Fig. 2(c), which indicates that the VB position of Bi2WO6 is 2.33 eV. Through the formula
E
V B - N H E
= Φ +
E
V B - X P S
- 4.44
, where Φ presents the electronic work function of the instrument (4.20 eV), the VB position of Bi2WO6 versus the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) was estimated to be 2.09 V [32]. According to the equation: Eg = EVB-ECB, the CB position of Bi2WO6 relative to the NHE was determined to be −0.63 V, thus obtaining the band structure diagram of Bi2WO6 (Fig. 2d). The work functions of N-CQDs (100) and Bi2WO6 (100) were calculated to be 4.79 and 5.93 eV using DFT, respectively (Fig. 2e and f). Obviously, before the coupling of N-CQDs and Bi2WO6, the Fermi level (EF) of N-CQDs is higher than that of Bi2WO6. After coupling N-CQDs and Bi2WO6, electrons in N-CQDs can be spontaneously transferred to Bi2WO6 until the EF at their interface reaches equilibrium. The surface of Bi2WO6 bends downwards due to the accumulation of electronic energy bands, forming a heterocomposite with N-CQDs, and establishing a built-in electric field (IEF) between N-CQDs and Bi2WO6 (Fig. 2g). Under illumination, the IEF can drive the transfer of photoproduced electrons from the CB of Bi2WO6 to N-CQDs, thereby achieving efficient separation of photoproduced carriers.

The SEM image of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM is shown in Fig. 3 (a), revealing the presence of numerous cross-linked pore structures on the surface of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM, with a large number of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 nanoparticles embedded within the PVDF-HFP matrix. As further evident from the SEM micrographs in Fig. 3(b and c), some particles are encapsulated within the PVDF-HFP matrix, while a larger number of nanoparticles remain exposed on the PVDF-HFP surface, displaying a morphology resembling spherical shapes. However, the SEM image of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 (Fig. S2) shows that its morphology is similar to the petal structure. This morphological difference may be due to PVDF-HFP covering the surface of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 during the membrane preparation process. Moreover, the element mapping of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM is shown in Fig. S3, which demonstrates that it is composed of six elements including Bi, W, O, F, C and N, which are uniformly distributed without any other impurities.

The crystalline structures of photocatalysts were explored by way of XRD. As presented in Fig. 3(d), the diffraction peaks at 18.2° and 20.3° are assigned to the α and β phases of PVDF, respectively [33]. Among the five crystal phases of PVDF (i.e., α, β, γ, δ, and ε phases), the β phase shows the highest piezoelectric properties [34]. The diffraction peak of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM at 20.3° is higher than that at 18.2°, indicating a greater content of β phase compared to α phase, suggesting the formation of more piezoelectric phases, which is conducive to enhancing piezo-photocatalytic performance [35]. For the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM, all diffraction peaks can be indexed to PVDF-HFP or N-CQDs/Bi2WO6, demonstrating the successful loading of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 onto PVDF-HFP. The FTIR further analyzed the surface structures of PVDF-HFP and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM. As shown in Fig. 3(e), compared to the PVDF-HFP membrane, the shape and position of absorption bands in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM remain largely unchanged. However, there were significant changes in the intensity of some absorption bands. Typically, the strong absorption bands at 841 and 758 cm−1 are assigned to the β-phase and α-phase of the PVDF. The content of β-phase in PVDF has a significant role in its piezoelectric properties. The β-phase content of PVDF-HFP and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM was evaluated using the following formula (Eq. (3)) [36]:
(3)
F
β
=
A β
K β
K α
A α
+
A β
where Aα and Aβ denote the absorbance at 758 and 841 cm−1, respectively. Kα and Kβ refer to the absorption coefficients with values of 6.1 × 104 and 7.7 × 104 cm2/mol, respectively. The content of β-phase for the PVDF-HFP and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM was 44.1 % and 44.0 %, respectively. The results show that the addition of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 had almost no effect on the content of β-phase in PVDF-HFP. Furthermore, Fig. 3(f) reveals that the surface contact angle of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM is 64°, indicating its favorable hydrophilicity. This may be attributed to the formation of a hydrogen bond network structure by F atoms in the polymer chain, thereby enhancing the hydrophilicity of the PVDF-HFP membrane [37]. The aforementioned results demonstrate the successful preparation of a three-dimensional porous hydrophilic CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM photocatalytic membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation performance of OTC by the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM
ce:section.ce:para: The removal efficiency of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM for OTC was evaluated by adjusting the stirring rate under visible light or darkness. The membrane was submerged in the OTC solution to sustain its mass transfer and vibration. The stirring rate of 0 r/min and visible light irradiation are designated as the static state, which is considered a photocatalytic reaction. As revealed in Fig. 4 (a), when immersed in a solution and exposed to irradiation for 90 min under visible light, its removal rate of OTC by N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM is only 63.1 %. When the water flow rate increases to 900 r/min, the removal rate of OTC by N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM reaches 85.3 %. It indicates that the removal rate of OTC by the membrane gradually increases with the increase of water flow rate under visible light. Compared with the performance of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 in degrading OTC (Fig. S4), the removal efficiency of OTC by N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM is slightly reduced under visible light and stirring, which is attributed to that PVDF-HFP covers the reaction sites on the surface of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 during the fabrication of the membrane.

Referring to the first-order kinetic reaction equation: ln(Ct/C0) = kt, the reaction rate constant (k) at different stirring rates was fitted. As revealed in Fig. 4(b and c), the membrane's corresponding rate constant k at a mechanical stirring of 900 r/min is 0.0260 min−1, which is 3.3-fold higher than removing OTC under static conditions. This may be due to the water flow-induced a piezoelectric field in the PVDF-HFP membrane, which improved the separation and migration of photoproduced carriers in the N-CQD/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite. At the dark condition, the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM showed low degradation activity for OTC at different stirring rates (Fig. 4d). At mechanical stirring of 300, 600, and 900 r/min, the corresponding rate constants k are 0.0011, 0.0013, and 0.0018 min−1, respectively (Fig. 4e and f). This phenomenon may be attributed to the low carrier density in the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite in the absence of light excitation. As given in Table 1 , the k value of piezoelectric photocatalysis is significantly greater than the sum of the k values of piezo-catalysis and photocatalysis, indicating that there is a favorable synergistic effect between photocatalysis and piezo-catalysis generated by fluid flow.

Compared with most previous PVDF-based piezo-photocatalytic membranes (Table S1), the N-CQDs/B2WO6@PHM photocatalytic membrane developed in this study exhibits favorable antibiotic removal efficiency under the synergistic effect of visible light and weak water flow. It is important that we address the issue of recycling powdery catalysts from wastewater and underline the potential of its integration with natural low-frequency water flows to purify contaminated water.

According to the above investigations, the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM exhibited favorable OTC removal performance under visible light and a water flow rate of 900 r/min. The reusability of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM during OTC degradation was also evaluated under the same conditions. As revealed in Fig. 5 (a), the degradation rate of OTC by N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM still maintained at 80 % after five cycles (only decreased by about 5 %), which is assigned to the favorable synergistic effect between photocatalysis and water flow-driven piezocatalysis. Moreover, the XRD spectra (Fig. 5b) show that the structure of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM does not significantly change after repeated uses. The SEM images (Fig. 5c and d) depict that the microstructure of N-CQD/Bi2WO6@PHM remains unchanged after multiple uses, maintaining its porous structure and still containing a large number of nanoparticles on its surface. Fig. S5 further proves that the particles on the surface of the PVDF-HFP membrane still belong to N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 after multiple uses for OTC degradation. The above findings imply that the designed N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM possess favorable reusability and stability.

The reactive species implicated in the photocatalytic removal of OTC over N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 were determined by employing EPR spectra. Specifically, the hydroxyl radical (
OH) and the superoxide radical (
O2 −), along with singlet oxygen (1O2), were detected by using DMPO and TEMP as trapping agents, respectively. 1O2 and
OH were detected in a H2O solvent, while the
O2 − was detected in a methanol solvent. As revealed in Fig. 6 (a, b and c), there is no signal of reactive species in the dark, but strong signals of DMPO-
OH, DMPO-
O2 −, and TEMP-1O2 are generated over N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 under visible light irradiation. Based on the conditions for the generation of reactive species, the mechanism of reactive species production by N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 was analyzed thermodynamically. Due to the VB position of Bi2WO6 being 2.09 V, it is corrected compared to OH−/
OH (1.99 V vs. NHE) [38]. In this case, h+ in Bi2WO6 VB can oxidize OH− to
OH. In addition, the CB position of Bi2WO6 is −0.63 V, which is lower than O2/
O2 − (−0.33 V vs. NHE) [39]. In this case, electrons in the CB of Bi2WO6 can reduce O2 to
O2 −. Besides, the
O2 − can react with h+ to generate 1O2. Due to the formed IEF at the interface between N-CQDs and Bi2WO6, the separation of photoproduced carriers was promoted. As a result, the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite generated abundant reactive species under visible light exposure including
O2 −, 1O2 and
OH.

To identify the contribution of reactive species in the removal of OTC over N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM under visible light and mechanical stirring, quenching experiments of reactive species were conducted. The AA, IPA, FFA and Na2C2O4 were employed to scavenge
O2 −,
OH, 1O2 and h+, respectively, with a concentration of 2 mmol/L for all scavengers. As displayed in Fig. 6(d), the addition of AA and FFA to the reaction system significantly inhibited the degradation rate of OTC, indicating that
O2 − and 1O2 are major reactive species for OTC degradation. Furthermore, after fitting the degradation curve with the first-order reaction model, the curve shown in Fig. 6(e) was obtained. When AA, FFA, IPA and Na2C2O4 were added to the OTC solution, the first-order reaction k of OTC degradation by N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM are 0.0078, 0.0142, 0.0251 and 0.0255 min−1, respectively (Fig. 6f). The contribution of reactive species to OTC degradation was determined by the following equations (Eqs. (4)–(7)) [40,41]:

The contribution rates of
O2 −, 1O2, h+ and
OH over OTC degradation were determined to be 69.8 %, 45.3 %, 9.4 %, and 3.3 %, respectively, indicating that the reactive species involved in OTC degradation are in the order of
O2 − > 1O2 > h+ >
OH. Due to the quenching of
O2 − and h+, which may affect the generation of 1O2, thus the sum of all relative contribution values is greater than 100 % [42].

The piezoelectric properties of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM were studied using PFM [43]. From the morphological image in Fig. 7 (a), it can be observed that there are uneven pore structures on the membrane surface, which are in line with the SEM-characterized morphology. The N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM show significant amplitude and phase piezoelectric response (Fig. 7b and c). When the applied voltage is inverted from +10 to −10 V, the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM presents a typical amplitude voltage butterfly curve accompanied by a significant phase angle shift of 180° (Fig. 7d and e). In addition, both the butterfly loop and the phase hysteresis loop are horizontally shifted, indicating that polarization is reversible. The PFM analysis demonstrates that the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM have favorable piezoelectric properties. In addition, the d33 of the PVDF-HFP membrane and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM were measured using the ZJ-3A quasi-static d33 measuring instrument. As shown in Fig. 7(f), the d33 values of PVDF-HFP membrane and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM are 4.8 and 4.6 pC/N, respectively, suggesting that the piezoelectric properties of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM primarily originates from PVDF-HFP membrane.

Furthermore, the variation of piezoelectric potential in PVDF-HFP under different pressures was simulated using COMSOL Multiphysics. The simulation size of PVDF-HFP is 20 × 150 × 1 nm3 and the polar axis of the simulated PVDF membrane is oriented along the y-axis. Other parameters utilized in the simulation can use the predetermined parameters in COMSOL Multiphysics. As shown in Fig. 8 , when PVDF-HFP is subjected to external pressure, its opposite side can generate positive and negative potentials. As the proportion of polarization planes increases, the potential value continuously increases, resulting in a larger piezoelectric potential. Under a pressure of 10 Pa, the piezoelectric potential generated on the surface of PVDF-HFP is 40 mV. When a cavitation pressure of 104 Pa was applied to the surface of PVDF-HFP, a maximum piezoelectric potential of 1.02 V was generated. Therefore, the mechanical force generated by water flow induces a piezoelectric field on the surface of PVDF-HFP, enhancing the separation of photogenerated carriers in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6.

The previous analysis confirms the presence of an intrinsic electric field in the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite, which drives the efficient separation of photoproduced carriers under visible light. The excellent piezoelectric properties of the PVDF-HFP membrane have been demonstrated through PFM, d33 testing and theoretical simulation. After the formation of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM, the piezoelectric potential of PVDF-HFP combined with the heterojunction effect of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 further enhances the directional separation of photoproduced charges. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), the PVDF-HFP membrane is subjected to external force caused by water flow, resulting in deformation and internal piezoelectric polarization phenomenon, causing positive and negative charges to appear on the two opposite surfaces of the PVDF-HFP membrane, respectively [44,45]. This offers a driving force to induce the directional migration of current carriers in the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite under illumination, with more photoproduced electrons migrating to the positive charge surface and holes migrating to the negative charge surface. Due to the random or periodic bending direction of the PVDF-HFP membrane, the direction of piezoelectric potential generation is constantly changing. Consequently, the synergistic effect of the IEF and the piezoelectric field enhances the migration and separation efficiency of photoproduced carriers in the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite. This results in the generation of more reactive species, such as
O2 −, 1O2, h+ and
OH, within the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM, thereby promoting the removal of OTC. As revealed in Fig. 9(b), the EIS curve of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM shows a smaller semi-circular arc under vibration (V), indicating that the piezoelectric effect generated by vibration is beneficial for the rapid transfer of interface charges [46]. Furthermore, compared with static illumination (L), the instantaneous photocurrent response curve of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM (Fig. 9c) shows a higher photocurrent signal under the combined action of V and L, fully demonstrating that the piezoelectric effect of PVDF-HFP membrane enhances the separation efficiency of photoproduced carriers in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 heterocomposite [47].

The hydrophilic and porous nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)/Bi2WO6@polyvinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene (PVDF-HFP) photocatalytic membrane was successfully constructed using a facile phase transformation method. Through theoretical calculations, piezo-response force microscopy and the maximum effective piezoelectric coefficient measurements support that the PVDF-HFP membrane (PHM) has favorable piezoelectric properties, and it can deform and generate a piezoelectric field under the driving force of water flow caused by mechanical stirring. Besides, due to the difference in work functions, an internal electric field (IEF) is generated at the interface between N-CQDs and Bi2WO6. The piezoelectric field and the IEF synergistically enhance the separation of photoproduced carriers in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6, thereby improving the oxytetracycline (OTC) removal efficiency over the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM membrane system. As a result, when the mechanical stirring rate was increased from 0 to 900 r/min, the degradation rate of OTC over N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM membrane increased by 3.3-fold under visible light. Compared to the previously reported piezoelectric photocatalytic system [48,49], the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6@PHM membrane can utilize the mechanical energy of water flow to generate a piezoelectric field, enhancing the photocatalytic removal efficiency of pollutants. This work provides insights into the efficient recovery of photocatalysts and the role of multi-field synergistic effects in enhancing photogenerated charge separation. How to maintain the effective absorption of light and expose more surface-active sites of catalysts fixed on PVDF-HFP remains a key focus of future research.
